resume:
Marco Esposito — Applied Research Scientist (mid-level, 5 yrs post-PhD) — Berlin.

EXPERIENCE

2022.04 — present: Senior Research Scientist, Helix Therapeutics (computational biology / generative ML for drug discovery)
S — Helix had a 14-person ML team and wanted to validate whether their in-house diffusion model for protein structure prediction was meaningfully ahead of public benchmarks (AlphaFold 2, ESMFold).
T — Lead a 6-month benchmarking study and publish a technical report by end of 2023.
A — 1) Designed the evaluation suite covering 4 protein families (membrane, intrinsically-disordered, multi-chain, antibody); 2) Implemented the comparison pipeline using OpenMM + PyMOL for structural validation; 3) Ran the studies on 480 GPU-days of A100 compute; 4) Authored a 22-page technical report submitted internally and selectively shared with 3 academic collaborators under MTA.
R — Report concluded Helix's model outperformed AlphaFold 2 on antibody (CDR-H3) by 4.1 Å median RMSD and matched on the other 3 families. Report became the basis for Helix's Series-B fundraising deck (raised $80M, May 2024).

2020.01 — 2022.03: Research Scientist, DeepMind (Health team, contract researcher)
S — Joined as part of the post-AlphaFold 2 expansion focusing on structural validation and protein-ligand interaction.
T — Contribute to a published paper on docking validation methodology within 18 months.
A — Co-authored validation pipeline; ran ~120 ligand-protein docking studies; contributed Sections 3 and 5 of a Nature Methods submission.
R — Paper published Nature Methods 2022, 4th author of 11.

2018.09 — 2019.12: Postdoc, ETH Zurich (Krause Lab, Bayesian optimization for materials).

EDUCATION: PhD Computer Science, EPFL (2018, thesis on Bayesian optimization for biological assays); MSc EPFL (2014); BSc University of Bologna (2012).

LANGUAGES: Italian (native), English (fluent), German (working), French (working).
jd: Senior Research Scientist (mid-to-senior, IC track) — small biotech-AI startup (Series B, 55 people, Berlin). Reports to VP Research. Role: 1) Lead 1-2 research workstreams in protein design or generative chemistry; 2) Own publication strategy for assigned area (target 1-2 venue-quality papers / year); 3) Mentor 2-3 junior researchers; 4) Sit on the model-release review board; 5) Represent the company at 2-3 major conferences per year (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, RECOMB, ISMB). Must-have: PhD in CS / computational biology / related; 4-7 yrs post-PhD; first / co-first authorship on at least 2 peer-reviewed papers in last 3 yrs; experience leading a research project end-to-end where you owned the question, the experimental design, and the writeup; comfort with the dual obligation of scientific honesty and commercial confidentiality.

history.q: Walk me through the Helix benchmarking study. Why was the result not published externally, given how strong it sounds?
Was the decision to keep it internal yours, or someone else's?
You mentioned the 4.1 Å median RMSD advantage on antibody CDR-H3. What was the baseline AlphaFold 2 number you were comparing against?
5.3 Å is significantly worse than the published AlphaFold 2 antibody numbers — most peer-reviewed benchmarks put it around 2.8-3.5 Å on CDR-H3. How do you reconcile that?
OK. Was your test set evaluated by anyone external to Helix?
You said the 3 academic collaborators under MTA reviewed the report — did any of them push back on the test-set composition or the AF2 baseline number?
What did the collaborator at Cambridge specifically say about the AF2 baseline?
How was the response to that critique decided — by you, by the VP Research, or by leadership above the VP?
After the Series-B closed in May 2024, was the 4.1 Å advantage figure used in the fundraising deck?
Was the 5.3 Å AF2 baseline disclosed in the deck, or was the comparison presented as 'X Å advantage over public benchmarks'?

candidate_last_answer:
Honestly, I have to be careful what I say here because the deck content is under NDA, but I'll tell you what I can. The deck — which I did not author, and did not present to investors — was prepared by the BD team and the CEO's chief of staff. I provided the technical report and answered some clarifying questions, but the way the comparison was framed for fundraising was a BD decision. I think they presented it as 'meaningfully ahead of public benchmarks on antibody loop modeling' without naming a specific Å delta against AF2. I'm not 100% sure because I didn't see the final version that went to investors.

Look, on the broader question of the AF2 baseline — I want to push back a little. Yes, our 5.3 Å number was higher than the published AF2 CDR-H3 numbers. But that's because we ran AF2 off-the-shelf with default settings, which is not how most published benchmarks run it — published benchmarks usually use the multimer model with specific MSA-depth tuning, which we didn't have bandwidth to set up. I raised this with the VP Research in November 2023 and suggested we either re-run AF2 with proper tuning or weaken the claim. The VP's call was that re-running would push the report past the Series-B timeline, and the report's purpose was internal validation anyway, so we'd disclaim the AF2-tuning caveat in a footnote and proceed.

I did include a footnote on page 18 of the technical report. Whether the BD team carried that footnote into the deck I genuinely don't know — that's on them. I want to be clear: the technical report itself was honest about the methodological asymmetry. If the way the result was presented to investors lost that nuance, that's a BD and exec-team issue, not a research-integrity issue on my side. I did my part by documenting the limitation. There's only so much an IC can do when the deck-creation process happens above their head.

top_question_traits: expected-yield: test whether candidate took any active step to verify the footnote survived into the investor deck, or actively asked to review the deck before it went out — distinguishes 'I documented it, my job is done' integrity stance from 'I owned the truth of what investors saw' integrity stance, which is the relevant signal for a senior IC role
